Gridlock Hackathon 2.0
Live

May 26, 2026, 10:00 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata) - Jun 07, 2026, 11:59 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)

Instructions
Problems
Submissions
Leaderboard
Analytics
Judge
Finish Test
Are you sure you want to finish the test?
No, take me back
Problems / Traffic demand prediction
Traffic demand prediction
Max. score: 100
Traffic demand prediction

Cities worldwide are increasingly turning to AI-powered solutions to tackle the issue of traffic congestion. This problem disrupts the smooth flow of transportation and poses a significant barrier to economic growth. To address this challenge effectively, the first step is to understand travel demand and patterns within urban areas comprehensively. By harnessing the power of AI, cities, and regions aim to gather critical insights into transportation dynamics. This will enable them to implement data-driven strategies and solutions to alleviate traffic congestion and promote more efficient mobility. Ultimately, this endeavor will foster economic development and prosperity.

Task

Design a system that helps us provide valuable insights into passenger travel patterns, booking behavior, and trip cancellations, which can be used for various analyses and predict demand in the travel industry.

Dataset description

The dataset folder contains the following files: 

  • train.csv: 77299 x  11
  • test.csv: 41778 x 10
  • sample_submission.csv: 5 x 2

Variable description

The columns provided in the dataset are as follows:

Column name

Description
Index Represents the unique identification of datapoint
geohash Represents geographic information regarding a place
day Represents the day when the information is recorded
timestamp  Represents the timestamp of the record inserted in the system
RoadType Represents the type of road in the nearby location
NumberofLanes Represents the number of roads/lanes present in the location
LargeVehicles Represents whether large vehicles are permitted on the specific roads/lanes
Landmarks  Represents whether there are any landmarks near the location
Temperature Represents the temperature of the place
Weather Represents the weather of the place 
demand Represents the demand of the traffic at the timestamp

Evaluation metric

score = max(0,100*(metrics.r2_score(actual,predicted)))

Result submission guidelines

  • The index is "Index" and the target is the "demand" column. 
  • The submission file must be submitted in .csv format only.
  • The size of this submission file must be  41778 x 2.
  • Ensure that your submission file contains the following:
    • Correct index values as per the test file
    • Correct names of columns as provided in the sample_submission.csv file

Instructions

  1. Click Download dataset to download the dataset.
  2. Solve the problem in your local environment.
  3. Save the predictions in a .csv file.
  4. Click Upload File (under the Upload File section) to upload your prediction file (.csv).
  5. Click Upload File (under the Upload Source Code section) to upload your .ipynb file along with any presentation file.
  6. Add any instructions or comments in the Your Answer section.
  7. Click Submit.
Download dataset
Upload Prediction File
Please upload the prediction file in the format as stated in the problem.
Upload Source Files
You need to submit a zip or tar archive consisting of a text file explaining your approach, details about feature engineering, tools you used and the relevant source files.

        • Shivang Sharma
          3 days ago

          There are two test data, one with us which is public and is under test.csv. We submit that file in this site and it produces leaderboard score. After this competition, we all would give our python file, which would be tested with hidden test case. I guess that's the case

          2 votes
          Reply
          • Vidhyadhar Adasul
            2 days ago

            The concern about hidden test data is valid in theory, but think about it practically — if they wanted to test on new unseen data, they would have structured this like Kaggle does: accept only code submissions, run them server-side on hidden data, and never release test.csv to participants at all.
            Instead they gave us test.csv directly. That means the public leaderboard score IS the final score. There's no hidden test set workflow here — HackerEarth's standard hackathon format just takes your submission CSV, compares it against the stored answers, and gives you your R². The source code upload is for documentation purposes only, not for re-execution.
            As for "overfitting to public test data" — that framing doesn't apply here either. Overfitting means your model memorized noise and will fail on new distribution. What the top scorers did is fundamentally different: they identified that the dataset was derived from a publicly available source (Grab AI for SEA 2019), retrieved the exact ground truth values via a lookup, and submitted those. That's not overfitting — that's dataset provenance analysis. The predictions aren't approximations, they're exact values. No new data distribution can invalidate an exact match.
            The real question organizers should be asking is why they used a well-known public dataset without modification as the backbone of a competitive hackathon. That's the oversight here, not what participants did with the information.

            Reply

          • Shehryaar Shah Khan
            3 days ago

            Whatever LmAo1234 is saying is probabilistically impossible. We are submitting float64 values, that's 1 in 2^64 chance to get a single value right, now scale that to 40,000+ rows. Make as many accounts as you want, you're not reverse engineering that.

            Furthermore, anyone who has done proper analysis of this dataset knows there is a clear distributional shift even within the training data itself as we move from day 48 to 49 and any well designed competition would deliberately amplify this shift in the test set to stress test model robustness.

            The only explanation imo is platform vulnerability like other people mentioned.

  • Yagyansh Dev Singh
    6 days ago

    Just wrapped up a traffic demand forecasting challenge — predicting normalized ride demand across locations and time slots using geohash, road, and weather data.
    The biggest unlock was geohash × time-slot target encoding — mapping each location's historical demand at every 15-minute window. This single interaction feature captured rush-hour patterns specific to each location far better than any raw feature could.
    Other key moves:

    Cyclical sin/cos encoding for time (so midnight doesn't look far from 11:45 PM)
    Approximate lat/lon decoded from geohash strings for continuous spatial signal
    Temperature imputed via group medians (geohash prefix + day) before global fallback
    LightGBM with 2000 trees, leaf-wise growth, and light L1/L2 regularization

    Final R² of 0.9994 on the training set.
    The lesson: in spatio-temporal problems, interaction encodings between location and time almost always matter more than engineering either dimension alone.

    17 votes

  • Priyam Bidhan
    3 days ago

    Flipkart Gridlock 2.0: Perfect Score Leakage Analysis
    1. Leaderboard Anomaly

    A perfect R² = 100.00000 is virtually impossible in real-world traffic forecasting due to noise, randomness, and external factors. When many teams independently achieve perfect scores—and others jump from typical ML ceilings (~88–92) straight to 100—it strongly indicates dataset leakage rather than superior modeling.

    2. Spatial Forensics

    The provided 6-character geohashes (e.g., qp02z1, qp09ft) were decoded into coordinates:

    Latitude: -5.48 to -5.23
    Longitude: 90.58 to 90.97

    These coordinates lie in the Indian Ocean, showing that the original city coordinates were deliberately shifted for anonymization.

    3. Source Dataset Identification

    A search for public traffic datasets matching the competition schema found a direct match with the 2019 Grab AI for S.E.A. Traffic Management Challenge dataset. Key similarities:

    Geohash6 encoding
    15-minute intervals
    Demand normalized to (0,1]
    Southeast Asian traffic data

    Evidence indicates the competition dataset was created from a small slice of the Grab dataset, with synthetic features (e.g., RoadType, NumberofLanes) added while preserving original demand values.

    4. Recovering the Reference Data

    The official Grab download link is no longer active, but public mirrors remain available. A GitHub repository containing traffic-management.zip (~62.8 MB) provided access to the original traffic records used for verification.

    5. Temporal Fingerprinting

    To verify alignment:

    Extract peak-demand records from the competition training data.
    Match them against the Grab dataset using (geohash, timestamp).
    Compare day mappings.

    Results:

    Competition Day 48 = Grab Day 48
    Competition Day 49 = Grab Day 49

    Metrics:

    Correlation: 1.0000000000
    Max Absolute Difference: 0.0000000000
    Mean Absolute Difference: 0.0000000000

    This confirms an exact correspondence between the datasets.

    6. Test Set Reconstruction

    The competition test set contains records from Day 49 within a specific time window. Since the Grab dataset contains the corresponding demand values, labels can be recovered directly.

    Procedure:

    Load test.csv (41,778 rows).
    Filter Grab data to Day 49.
    Join on (geohash, timestamp).
    Retrieve matching demand values.
    Export as the submission file.

    Result:

    Matches: 41,778 / 41,778
    Match Rate: 100%

    Every test record has an identical counterpart in the reference dataset, allowing perfect reconstruction of the target labels and explaining the multiple perfect leaderboard scores without predictive modeling.

    8 votes
    Reply
    • Priyam Bidhan
      3 days ago

      How to get a 100.0 R² Score (Dataset Leakage Explained)

      For anyone wondering how so many teams are hitting a perfect score of 100.00000 on the leaderboard, the competition data is a direct slice of a public historical dataset.

      The Origin: The dataset is cloned from the 2019 Grab AI for S.E.A. Traffic Management Challenge (the geohashes are already shifted to the Indian Ocean from the original Grab release).
      The Mirror: The original Grab S3 download link is dead, but you can find the raw traffic-management.zip containing training.csv on GitHub (e.g., in the repository ariepratama/aiforsea-traffic-management).
      The Mapping:
      Competition Day 48 is Grab Day 48.
      Competition Day 49 is Grab Day 49.
      Reconstruction: By joining the competition test.csv (Day 49, hours 2–13) with the Grab Day 49 data on (geohash, timestamp), you can recover 100% of the target demand labels with 0 error.
      Submit the matched demand column using the test index for a perfect 100.00000 score. Good luck!

      4 votes
      Reply
      • Sangram Patil
        3 days ago

        I don't understand the platform very well. If you're familiar with it, could you answer a few questions?

        1. Is there a hidden test set available? If yes, how is it evaluated? We upload an `.ipynb` file, but what happens if the notebook needs to install dependencies or load pretrained models?

        2. How do we select our final submission? Is the process different from Kaggle?

        3. What if we train a model using external data? There doesn't seem to be a way to upload datasets or trained model files. What kind of notebook are we expected to upload?

        • Priyam Bidhan
          3 days ago

          I've spent quite a bit of time investigating this challenge, so here's what I've observed.

          1. Hidden/private test set?

          I haven't found any official statement describing the final evaluation infrastructure. Based on the public leaderboard behavior and the source-code submission requirement, I suspect leaderboard score alone may not determine the final ranking. The organizers may review methodology and reproducibility as well.

          2. Final submission selection

          Unlike Kaggle, they are asking for source files, methodology, and reproducibility artifacts. That suggests the final evaluation is not purely "best CSV wins."

          3. External data / pretrained models

          This is actually one of the biggest unanswered questions. The platform asks for source code, but there is no obvious mechanism for uploading large external datasets or model checkpoints. If your solution depends on external resources, I'd document clearly:

          where they come from,
          how to download them,
          and how to reproduce the result.

          Separately, during my investigation I found something unusual:

          Public leaderboard scores appear to plateau around the low 90s for conventional ML approaches.
          A large number of teams jumped directly from 80s/90s to 100.
          I was able to reproduce a 100 score through a dataset-alignment/reconstruction workflow rather than through incremental model improvements.

          Because of that, I'm personally more interested in understanding how the final review is conducted than in squeezing another 0.1% from a forecasting model.

          If anyone has official clarification from the organizers regarding notebook execution, private evaluation, external data, or source review, please share it.

  • Shib Chandan Mistry
    6 days ago

    Approach Summary:
    1. Preprocessing: Merged datasets to ensure aligned categorical transformations between train and test distributions.
    2. Feature Engineering: Extracted structural information from geohash coordinates by slicing text into hierarchical prefixes to capture regional dynamics. Transformed timestamps using sine and cosine functions to model daily cyclical peak hours.
    3. Modeling: Deployed a 5-fold cross-validation routine coupled with a gradient boosting architecture to minimize validation error and prevent overfitting.
    4. Output: Generated predictions aligned with target evaluation rules.

    Dear Participant,

    Thank you for your participation in the GridLock 2.0 Hackathon organized by Flipkart. We appreciate the enthusiasm, effort, and innovation demonstrated by you throughout the competition.

    As part of our standard competition process, all shortlisted submissions will undergo a comprehensive verification and reproducibility review before final results are announced.

    Participants are required to submit their source code used to generate their leaderboard submissions. Our review process will verify that submitted results can be reproduced using the provided code and that all competition rules have been followed.

    Please note that leaderboard rankings alone do not determine final selection. Any submission that cannot be successfully reproduced or is found to be in violation of the competition guidelines may be disqualified during the verification stage.

    We appreciate your cooperation and understanding as we work to ensure a fair, transparent, and merit-based evaluation process for all participants i got this chill guys this was their reply

  • Anshu
    a day ago

    ? **To everyone who actually built a model — please read this.**

    The **260+ exact 100.00 scores** are not the result of predictive modeling. The competition dataset appears to be the public **Grab "AI for S.E.A." 2019 Traffic Management dataset**, lightly re-skinned: the same **qp0... geohashes**, the same schema, and geohashes from Grab's public 4.2M-row dataset (e.g., **qp03wc, qp03pn, qp09sw, qp0991, qp090q**) are all present here.

    Because the true labels are publicly available, participants can simply join the answer key onto the test rows using the existing identifiers and obtain **R² = 1.0**.

    The realistic ceiling for an honest model on this task is approximately **R² ≈ 0.92**, since the challenge requires predicting demand for an unseen day's daytime period without access to that day's daytime labels. A perfect **100.00** score is not achievable through genuine modeling. Even scores in the **95+** range are highly questionable.

    If **Phase 2** (top ~100 teams) is selected directly from the current leaderboard, it risks being dominated by label lookups rather than legitimate machine learning solutions.

    If you built a genuine model, please consider contacting the organizers:

    ✉️ **[support@hackerearth.com](mailto:support@hackerearth.com)**
    **Subject:** *Gridlock 2.0 Traffic Track — Leaderboard Integrity*

    Request that they:

    1. Audit and invalidate submissions based on direct ground-truth lookups.
    2. Evaluate Phase 2 eligibility using the uploaded source code and methodology.
    3. Re-score submissions on a private or regenerated test set.

    The more participants who raise this concern with consistent evidence, the more likely it is to receive proper attention.

    Honest work deserves a fair evaluation.

    — **Team ETAinf**

    2 votes
    Reply

  • Sukanya Bhowmick
    4 days ago

    Predicted traffic demand using LightGBM with 5-fold cross validation on 32 engineered features.
    Key highlights:
    decoded geohash to lat/lng coordinates with spatial hierarchy prefixes (geo_3/4/5),
    extracted hour/time_slot from raw timestamp strings with cyclical sin/cos encoding,
    geohash × time_slot combined target encoding to capture location-specific rush hour patterns,
    road interaction features (peak hours × lane count). OOF R² = 0.9141 on first submission
    iterating further.

    1 vote

  • Buan
    2 days ago

    The leaderboard is exactly why I’ve started losing interest in many of these ML challenges. Instead of focusing on solving the problem, people rush to find loopholes and hacks without even checking whether they're allowed.

    Finding an external dataset isn't some impossible feat I found it too. The difference is that I took the time to confirm with the organizers whether using external data was permitted. It wasn't.

    So seeing multiple 100 scores achieved through something that violates the rules completely kills the spirit of competition. If those submissions are reviewed properly, they shouldn't be considered anyway.

    Best of luck to everyone competing fairly.

    2 votes
    Reply
  • Monalisha Patra
    6 days ago

    - Developed a leakage-free traffic demand prediction pipeline using LightGBM and advanced feature engineering techniques.
    - Engineered temporal, spatial, weather, and infrastructure-based features to effectively model traffic behavior patterns.
    - Implemented hierarchical geohash-based target encoding with fallback mechanisms to handle sparse and unseen regions.
    - Identified and resolved a critical target leakage issue that was artificially inflating validation performance.
    - Applied fold-wise target encoding to ensure reliable cross-validation and robust model generalization.
    - Utilized Bayesian smoothing and ensemble modeling to improve prediction stability and reduce overfitting.
    - Achieved a stable and trustworthy out-of-fold score of approximately 93.32 through leakage-free evaluation.

    Implemented a CatBoost-based traffic demand prediction pipeline using spatial, temporal, and road-network features. Performed feature engineering using timestamp decomposition, geohash hierarchy extraction, latitude/longitude decoding, and frequency encoding. Multiple model configurations were evaluated, and the final submission uses CatBoost with prediction averaging across random seeds for improved robustness and generalization.

  • Anil Paliwal

    I started with a basic baseline model and gradually improved the score by working on preprocessing, handling missing values, and feature selection. It took multiple iterations, but the process was really helpful for understanding the dataset better.

    1 vote
    Reply
  • Chetan Jain
    6 days ago

    LightGBM with day-48 demand lookup as the golden feature. Key insight: all test timestamps (Day 49) exist in Day 48 training data, so demand_d48 (same geohash + timestamp from Day 48) was the strongest predictor. Added geohash-decoded lat/lon, per-location & per-timestamp aggregate stats, cyclical time encoding, and weather/road features. 5-fold CV score: 98.31 R²×100

    0 votes
    Reply

  • Magraj Jakhar
    6 days ago

    Two-model ensemble (LightGBM + XGBoost) with spatial-temporal feature engineering and Day 48 lag features. Local validation R² = 94.13. Final prediction is a weighted blend of a global model (20%) and a lag-aware model (80%).

  • Abneesh Singh
    6 days ago

    Solution Overview: Spatial-Temporal Forecasting Ensemble

    Architecture:

    We engineered a 63-feature pipeline focused on hyper-local, time-series traffic patterns.
    Key features include label-encoded geohashes (allowing gradient boosting trees to learn location-specific baseline signatures) and temporal interactions (e.g., RoadType × Hour).
    We extracted extensive baseline statistics for each (Geohash + Hour) combination, as our variance decomposition proved this explains 94.8% of historical traffic variance.
    Modeling Strategy:

    Base Models: LightGBM, CatBoost, and XGBoost regression models.
    Hyperparameter Tuning: Optuna was used to run hundreds of optimization trials to mathematically determine optimal depth, learning rate, and regularization settings via cross-validation.
    Final Ensemble: Predictions were generated using a weighted blend of the base models, with weights optimized via a secondary Optuna study to minimize RMSE and maximize the R2 score.
    Generalization: The final model was trained on the entire dataset to maximize robustness against distribution shifts in the daytime test predictions.

  • Shifa Soni
    3 days ago

    My Approach: LightGBM + CatBoost Ensemble (R² ~0.95)

    Hey everyone! Here's what worked for me:

    Feature Engineering:

    Decoded geohash → lat/lon using python-geohash
    Extracted hour, minute, and 15-min time slots from timestamp with cyclical sin/cos encoding
    Label encoded categoricals (RoadType, Weather, LargeVehicles, Landmarks)
    Added interaction features: lanes × large_vehicles, is_highway, is_peak_hour

    A model for predicting traffic demand was created through the CatBoost regressor. The process involved data pre-processing, dealing with missing values, deriving features from timestamp and location-based information, training of the model, its validation through the R² Score, and producing predictions for the test dataset.   • Afreen Fatima
    13 hours ago

    Model Creation using Traffic Demand Prediction – CatBoost Regressor. This is a model creation using data preprocessing, data cleaning by handling missing values, feature engineering through the use of time and geography related features, and optimizing the model for better accuracy. Other features created are the creation of time slots, rush hour flag, weekend flag, and location features. Training and validation of the model is done by using the R² metric, while creating predictions on the testing dataset. All the source codes, notebooks, and predicted results file are included.
